explanation.text: Paragraph 5 acknowledges that stunt work is hard and dangerous, but it also says performers enjoy its thrill and push themselves toward harder stunts. Their enjoyment explains why they willingly accept the job's risks.

Paragraph 10 says stunt actors must be fit and strong and often use gymnastics or martial arts to develop flexibility and learn safe falls. These examples support the broader answer that they train their bodies for action and good physical condition.

Stunt actors work wherever a movie or television program is being made, including remote deserts, jungles, and mountains. Because filming can continue far from home for weeks or months, the performers must travel and stay there too.

Paragraph 13 explains that circus acts are performed to music and that its rhythm gives performers cues for each section. Those cues let performers know when to begin and end the different parts of an act.

Paragraphs 15 through 19 name falls, sports injuries, fires, explosions, and accidents as risks of stunt work. These examples support the author's points by giving specific details about the dangers performers face.

The article mainly explains the work and lives of stunt actors and circus performers. Paragraph 7 best supports that idea because it identifies both kinds of performers and describes the important difference between their roles.

Ms. Snoops writes a large "M" as a reminder so that she will not lose her wonderful idea. Even with the note, she cannot remember what the letter means, which reveals that she sometimes forgets things.
Ali later says that she plans to become an archaeologist, someone who studies objects from the past. Her habit of collecting old objects and asking about their histories best reveals that interest.

Ms. Snoops says she would gather pieces of old stories and then "fill in the holes" herself. Her next comment that writers make things up shows that the phrase means creating details to complete an unfinished story.

Ms. Snoops says she enjoyed old stories and invented missing details, and Ali replies that she likes to do the same thing. Ali also admits that making things up can be fun, which shows that both characters enjoy creating stories.

The rusty tin and broken doll head may look like discarded objects, but Ms. Snoops recognizes the doll as dear Shirley and reacts with delight. This contrast supports the theme that an object that seems worthless can be special to someone.

Ali's treasures restore Ms. Snoops's memories and help her remember her plan to write memoirs about Orange Street. This detail belongs in a summary because it states the central result of their visit and resolves the forgotten-"M" mystery.

Larry Greene is an exercise science expert, a former professional distance runner and coach, and an author on training. These qualifications show that he has knowledge and experience, so readers can trust his running advice.

A good coach can teach proper form, help prevent injuries, and correct a runner's pace. Joining a team or club is therefore helpful because it gives a runner access to coaching that is difficult to replace alone.

The "Fun Run" box suggests playful movements and new exercises to do after each lap. These are extra training ideas that add to the main article's advice about preparing and gradually building running ability.

Paragraph 5 tells runners to begin with a short distance and slowly add more over several weeks. This gradual increase supports the claim because it gives the body time to adjust and build the endurance to run farther.

Paragraph 6 advises a partner to check a runner's posture and running form, while the article also stresses proper pacing. In this context, running "efficiently" means using correct speed and form so effort is not wasted.

The article's sections explain how to prepare with coaching, gradual distance increases, and safe running habits. Greene's statement about preparing properly best expresses that overall idea because it includes both strong performance and feeling good.

Demian trains regularly, qualifies for a national event, and wins his age group's race after joining a running program. His progress and increased confidence demonstrate that racing goals can encourage a person to work hard.
